snippets.text:
Gene editing may refer to:Genetic engineering of any organism by genome editing. Gene editing is the emerging molecular biology technique that makes very specific targeted changes by insertion, deletion or substitution of genetic material in an organism's DNA to obtain desired results. Examples of gene editing are CRISPR, zinc finger nuclease, transcription activator-like effector nuclease (TALEN), oligonucleotide directed mutagenesis + meganucleases.
Genome editing, a type of genetic engineering
Gene therapy, the therapeutic delivery of nucleic acid polymers into a patient's cells as a drug to treat disease
CRISPR gene editing, a genetic engineering technique. CRISPR are termed as SDN since they target a specific part of the genome. There are 3 different categories of SDN. SDN1 makes random mutations at the target site to repair the damaged host DNA without involving any foreign DNA. SDN2 uses small non coding homologous repair DNA to achieve a specific nucleotide sequence to repair the host DNA by HDR, which is a natural nucleic acid repair system. SDN3 uses a large stretch of protein coding donor DNA, which is targeted for insertion through HDR at a predefined genomic locus.
TALEN editing, using transcription activator-like effector nucleases. TALENs are another type of genome editing tool. They work by using engineered proteins that can recognize and bind to specific DNA sequences, which then trigger a cut in the DNA. TALENs are less efficient than CRISPR/Cas9, but they are still a useful tool for genome editing.
Zinc finger editing, using zinc finger nucleases
Natural genetic engineering (NGE) has been proposed by molecular biologist James A. Shapiro to account for novelty created in the course of biological evolution.
snippets.title: Gene editing

facts.claim: CRISPR is a genetic engineering technique that makes specific targeted changes in an organism's DNA.
CRISPR gene editing is categorized as SDN, which targets a specific part of the genome.
SDN1 makes random mutations at the target site to repair damaged host DNA without involving foreign DNA.
SDN2 uses small non-coding homologous repair DNA to achieve a specific nucleotide sequence to repair host DNA.
SDN3 uses a large stretch of protein coding donor DNA targeted for insertion through HDR at a predefined genomic locus.

final_report:
# CRISPR-Cas9 System: Mechanisms, Applications, and Ethical Considerations

## Executive Summary

This report explores the CRISPR-Cas9 system, focusing on its key components, mechanisms for gene editing, medical applications, and ethical considerations. The findings highlight the system's targeted approach to genetic modifications and its implications in medicine.

## What are the key components of the CRISPR-Cas9 system?

- CRISPR is a genetic engineering technique that enables specific targeted changes in an organism's DNA. [Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_editing)
- CRISPR gene editing is categorized as SDN (Site-Directed Nucleases), which targets a specific part of the genome. [Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_editing)

## How does the CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism facilitate gene editing?

- SDN1 makes random mutations at the target site to repair damaged host DNA without involving foreign DNA. [Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_editing)
- SDN2 uses small non-coding homologous repair DNA to achieve a specific nucleotide sequence to repair host DNA. [Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_editing)
- SDN3 uses a large stretch of protein coding donor DNA targeted for insertion through HDR (Homology-Directed Repair) at a predefined genomic locus. [Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_editing)

## What are the primary medical applications of CRISPR-Cas9 technology?

- The report does not provide specific details on the primary medical applications of CRISPR-Cas9 technology.

## What ethical considerations arise from the use of CRISPR-Cas9 in medicine?

- The report does not provide specific details on the ethical considerations arising from the use of CRISPR-Cas9 in medicine.

## Limitations

- The scope of this research is limited to biological mechanisms, medical applications, and ethical considerations of CRISPR-Cas9.
- Non-medical applications and detailed technical protocols for gene editing are not covered. 
- Specific examples of medical applications and ethical considerations are not provided in the available facts.
